sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 57-year-old male, presents with a 2-week history of shortness of breath, which occurs both at rest and with exertion.
He reports coughing up blood for the past 3 weeks, with an estimated 3-4 cups of blood per day.
He has a history of diabetes, high blood pressure, and asthma.
The patient's blood sugar has been running high, with readings over 200 after meals and around 150 in the morning, despite wearing a dexcom and taking metformin 500mg twice a day.
He also takes Norvasc, but has been holding off on taking it due to low blood pressure readings.
The patient has a history of knee problems, but no swelling or bruising.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes, high blood pressure, and asthma.
He has been taking metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes and Norvasc for high blood pressure.
Review of systems: The patient reports coughing up blood, but denies any fever, bruising, or bleeding from other areas.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes
Norvasc (hold for now)
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's oxygen saturation is 94% on pulse oximetry, and his blood pressure is 120/70 mmHg.
Physical exam:
Lung exam reveals bilateral rails and rhonchi on the right side, with diminished breath sounds on the right side.
Heart exam shows a 2/6 systolic ejection murmur, which is unchanged from before.
Belly exam is soft and non-tender.
Extremity exam shows 1+ nonpitting edema on both lower extremities.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with a possible pulmonary infection or another underlying condition causing his shortness of breath and coughing up blood.
Problem list:
Diabetes mellitus; Hypertension; Asthma; Cough with hemoptysis; Shortness of breath; Pulmonary infection suspected; Blood clot or another underlying condition suspected.
Plan:
I will order a chest x-ray and refer the patient to the emergency department for further evaluation and blood testing.
I will also order a CT scan of the chest to evaluate for a blood clot or another underlying condition.
I will hold off on the patient's Norvasc until further evaluation of his blood pressure is complete.
Once the patient is stable, we will reassess his blood pressure and adjust his treatment plan accordingly.
I will also order another hemoglobin A1c to evaluate his diabetes control.
Follow-up:
I will have the patient return in 3 weeks to discuss his diabetes treatment plan and adjust his medication as needed.
